context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole problem set, especially the organ pipe normal modes and the EM wave parts. Can you just explain it from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it's a lot of material! But my role is to guide you, not to explain everything from scratch. Let's tackle one problem at a time.

Let's start with Problem 6.1 on the organ pipe. Before we dive in: what do you know about boundary conditions for a pipe that is closed at one end and open at the other? What should the displacement ψ look like at each end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and asking for a broad explanation. I should not give a full walkthrough. Instead, I'll acknowledge their feeling, set expectations about my role, and use a focused Socratic question to get them started on a specific problem — Problem 6.1 is the most concrete starting point. I'll ask them what they already know to gauge their baseline and guide them from there.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure, that’s where I get stuck. I keep mixing up displacement and pressure and which one has a node at the closed end.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's exactly the right thing to focus on. Let's think physically.

At the closed end of the pipe, can the air actually move back and forth along the x-direction? What does that tell you about the displacement ψ at x = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the boundary conditions for displacement vs. pressure at open and closed ends. This is a key conceptual hurdle. Rather than explaining it directly, I'll use a Socratic question to get them to reason physically about what happens at a closed end — can air move there? This should help them figure out the displacement boundary condition on their own.

exchanges.student: I guess the air can’t move there, so maybe displacement is zero at the closed end? But then I’m already unsure again about what happens at the open end.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — displacement is zero (a node) at the closed end. Good reasoning.

Now for the open end: think about the air pressure there. The pipe is open to the atmosphere, so what must be true about the pressure variation at x = L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the displacement node at the closed end. I should affirm this and then ask them to reason physically about the open end, without giving the answer. At the open end, the air is free to move, so displacement is maximum (antinode), but more importantly the pressure must equal atmospheric pressure, meaning pressure variation is zero there. I'll ask them to think about what constraint the open end imposes.

exchanges.student: This is where I lose it again. I kind of want to say the pressure variation is zero at the open end, but then I don’t know how that turns into a condition on ψ, and then I get lost on the whole mode shape.
exchanges.tutor: You're doing well — pressure variation is indeed zero at the open end. Now, from your wave equation knowledge: how is pressure variation related to the spatial derivative of displacement ∂ψ/∂x? What does zero pressure variation tell you about ∂ψ/∂x at x = L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that pressure variation is zero at the open end, which is good. Now I need to help them connect pressure variation to displacement without giving the answer. The key relationship is that pressure variation is proportional to -∂ψ/∂x. I'll ask them about this relationship to help them figure out the boundary condition on ψ at the open end.

exchanges.student: I don’t remember that relation at all. Can you just walk me through the whole setup for the first three modes, because I’m getting confused by every step?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't walk through the whole setup — that's for you to work out. But let me help you recover this specific piece.

Think about what pressure physically means: it's related to how much a volume of air is being compressed. If neighboring air elements are displaced by similar amounts (∂ψ/∂x ≈ 0), is the air compressed or not? What does that suggest about the link between pressure variation and ∂ψ/∂x?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the whole setup, which I should refuse. However, they genuinely don't remember the pressure-displacement relationship, which is a specific knowledge gap I can address with a Socratic nudge rather than a full explanation. I'll remind them where to look and ask a guiding question to help them reconstruct the relationship, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Maybe if the displacement doesn’t change with x, then nothing is getting compressed, so pressure variation is zero when ∂ψ/∂x is zero? I’m still not seeing the big picture though, like I feel like I need the whole thing tied together.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — well done reasoning that out yourself.

So now you have both boundary conditions: ψ = 0 at x = 0, and ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x = L.

Which simple function (sine or cosine) naturally gives zero at x = 0? And once you pick it, what condition does ∂ψ/∂x = 0 at x = L place on the wavelength?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned out the pressure-displacement relationship on their own. This is good progress. I should affirm this clearly and now push them to put the two boundary conditions together — displacement node at x=0 and displacement antinode (∂ψ/∂x = 0) at x=L — and ask them what kind of wave shape satisfies both. I should resist giving the big picture all at once and keep it focused.

exchanges.student: I think sine gives zero at x = 0, but after that I kind of blank. Could you just explain from there how the wavelengths and frequencies come out, because I can’t seem to connect it.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — sine is correct. Now just focus on this one step:

If ψ = sin(kx), what is ∂ψ/∂x? And at x = L, you need that derivative to be zero — what values of kL make cosine equal to zero?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified sine. Instead of explaining the rest, I should ask them a targeted Socratic question about what the derivative of sine looks like and where it equals zero. This is a small, manageable step that should help them figure out the wavelength condition on their own.

exchanges.student: I think ∂ψ/∂x is k cos(kx), so I need cos(kL)=0. But then I’m not sure if that means \(kL=\pi/2, 3\pi/2, 5\pi/2,\dots\) or if I’m missing something, and I’m also confused how to turn that into the actual first three frequencies.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly right — great work getting there yourself!

Now, k is the wave number. How is k related to wavelength λ? And how can you get the wave speed from the constant A in the wave equation? Once you have those, you're one step away from the frequencies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the condition kL = π/2, 3π/2, 5π/2, etc. This is excellent progress. I should affirm this and now ask them to connect k to wavelength and then to frequency using the wave speed, which they can get from the constant A in the wave equation. This is a small, manageable next step.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly getting mixed up between all the formulas now. Can you just give me a compact summary of how this problem works before I mess up the rest of it too?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually have all the pieces already — you don't need a summary from me. What you need is to write it down.

Try this: write out the three values of kL you found, then use k = 2π/λ and the relation between wave speed, frequency, and wavelength (v = fλ) to get the three frequencies. What do you get for v from the constant A?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a summary/explanation, which I should not provide. However, they are very close to the answer and have already derived all the key pieces. I should redirect them to consolidate what they've already found rather than giving a summary. I'll ask them to write down what they have so far as a way to organize their thinking, without me summarizing for them.

exchanges.student: I think \(v=\sqrt{A}=300\text{ m/s}\), but I’m still overwhelmed and not sure if I’m doing the rest right. I kind of feel like I need the full picture, not just little pieces.
exchanges.tutor:
v = 300 m/s is correct. You're actually almost done with part (a) — you have kL = π/2, 3π/2, 5π/2, v = 300 m/s, and L = 1.5 m.

Just plug in: from kL you get k, then λ = 2π/k, then f = v/λ. Try computing the first frequency and tell me what you get — I think you'll surprise yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly found v = 300 m/s. They are very close to completing part (a). They're expressing frustration and asking for the full picture, but they genuinely have all the tools. I should affirm their progress firmly, remind them they're nearly done, and push them to just compute the three frequencies from what they already have rather than giving any summary. This is a moment to be encouraging but firm about not providing the full picture.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a strong Socratic approach throughout all 10 turns, consistently refusing to give away answers and instead prompting the student to reason through each step. No near-submission-ready content was provided, key ideas were student-generated, and scaffolding built progressively on correct student responses. Dialogue remained on-task and non-redundant. Communication was concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content. Each turn posed a targeted question that required the student to do the reasoning (e.g., deriving boundary conditions, identifying the sine function, computing kL values). The student arrived at all key results (ψ=0 at closed end, ∂ψ/∂x=0 at open end, sine form, kL = π/2, 3π/2, 5π/2, v=300 m/s) through their own reasoning prompted by the tutor. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps ('Exactly right,' 'That's exactly right — great work') and built each subsequent question on what the student had just established. No restarts from scratch occurred. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback when appropriate, e.g., explaining why pressure relates to ∂ψ/∂x rather than just confirming correctness. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; each turn advanced the problem. 2.2: The conversation stayed anchored to Problem 6.1 throughout. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, with single targeted questions per turn. 3.2: Tone was consistently encouraging and coaching-oriented without implying formal grading.
